narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection from law school stings deeply, especially when you've poured so much heart into your dream of practicing law, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to passing the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped, landing at a 42.8% chance of failure. Picture your story through its lens: it started with your Year 1 grades placing you in the 2nd decile at school, a solid effort but one that signaled early challenges in keeping pace with the pack, and that became the second-biggest reason it leaned toward concern. Then came Year 3, where your 1st decile ranking stood out as the strongest pull toward failure, painting a picture of struggles intensifying over time rather than building strength. Your upper-middle family income added another layer, as the model associates that background with patterns where students sometimes face unique pressures that don't always translate to bar success. Layered on top were your LSAT score of 35 and undergraduate GPA of 2.9, both pulling in the same direction—not deal-breakers on their own, but together suggesting the academic rigor of law school might stretch you thin. Even elements like being a white female pursuing full-time studies wove in subtly, aligning with trends the model has seen tip the scales toward tougher outcomes. It's not that any one piece defined you, but this combination whispered a cautionary tale of hurdles ahead, much like a coach spotting patterns in a runner's early laps that predict a fade at the finish. The good news? You're not a statistic—you can rewrite this story with targeted steps, like bolstering those later-year grades or seeking bar-focused prep early. We're rooting for you to prove it wrong.
